steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The skill is Command of Evidence (completing a logical conclusion). The decisive clue is the explicit constraint: a surface formed in a mostly basaltic environment "would be unlikely to contain large amounts of silica" — yet Laurent's data found high silica concentrations. The conclusion must resolve this contradiction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the information about silica concentrations collected by the spacecraft is likely more reliable than the silica information gleaned from infrared wavelengthss detected from Mars's surface.: the passage never compares the reliability of the two data sources — Laurent used both. This conclusion has no basis in the text. ✗
  • Choice B — high silica concentrations on Mars likely formed from a different process than that which formed the crusts of other planets.: the passage never mentions other planets' crusts. This introduces an unsupported comparison. ✗
  • Choice C — having a clearer understanding of the composition of Mars's crust and the processes by which it formed will provide more insight into how Earth's crust formed.: Earth's crust is never mentioned anywhere in the passage. This is an unjustified leap. ✗
  • Choice D — Mars's crust likely formed as a result of other major geological events in addition to the cooling of a magma ocean.: cooling magma produces basalt, not high-silica rock. Since high silica is present, additional processes beyond the magma ocean must have shaped the crust — this follows directly from the evidence. ✓
That is the correct answer.
